sentences.text: My given name was Dorrit Goldberger.
I was born in Vienna, Austria.
I was born in 1924 in Vienna and the daughter of a prominent businessman.
His name was Dolek Goldberger.
And my mother, her maiden name was Fanny Lissianski.
And there was my sister Liesl.
And we had a very gracious life.
We lived in a beautiful apartment that we owned.
And it was similar to what you have here in condominiums, so we owned a certain segment of the building.
And my father had two stores in Vienna in the clothing business.
He was a very highly respected and quite well-to-do.
And--
No, he was born in Zywiec in Poland, and he came to Vienna at an early age.
And he came from a very Orthodox Jewish family, which, later on in life, he changed.
He did not stay an Orthodox Jew.
And my mother was very conservative, and we were raised in the Jewish tradition, in the Conservative Jewish tradition.
We didn't--
No, my mother was born in Odessa in Russia, and she came to Vienna during the pogrom with her family.
And they were able to take most of their money out of Russia at that time, which was unusual, which enabled my mother to have a very genteel life.
She was raised in a very genteel way-- boarding schools and finishing schools.
And so her aim in life was to be a wonderful mother and house-- housewife.
Of course, she never cooked.
My mother never cooked.
And so we used to have a governess at home and-- in order to learn languages and, of course, a cook.
And--
Yes.
But the first time I ate with them was when I was six years old, because we were not permitted at the dining room table unless our manners were impeccable.
And we were really taught how to eat properly, and it was a very important thing to eat properly and behave yourself at a dinner table.
And most of the conversations were conducted at the dinner table, which really was lunchtime.
Between 12:00 and 2:00 in the afternoon, my father used to come home from business, and we had dinner.
And then maybe he took a little nap, and he left again and then didn't return until about 7:30 or 8 o'clock at night.
By that time, most of the time we were either already in bed, or we only had about 5 or 10 minutes to spend with my father.
And there was one thing that my father always used to preach to us.

And because, you know, my mother having gone through the pogrom and him also being in Poland and having experienced quite a number of things, he said, the most important thing in life that you can do is further and educate yourself, because they can take any-- everything away from you, all your possessions.
There's one thing they cannot take away, and that's what you have in your brain.
So learn, learn, learn.
And that was practically preached to us.
So learning and education was a very important facet in our life and, of course, also the closeness of the Jewish family.
And my mother was really the one that furthered it more so than my father, because, for some reason or the other, the Conservative religious was not his thing, and he turned against it.
So we were raised in-- not in a kosher home, but all the holidays were kept.
And--
All of them.
And we-- every Saturday we went to synagogue for the children services.
That was part of it.
My father didn't go, but my mother saw to it that we did go.
And of course, Rosh Hashanah and-- and Yom Kippur was-- nobody [? ate. ?] My-- my mother would not permit it.
But we did not keep a kosher home, though, because in Europe, in-- in Austria, especially in Vienna, it was very cosmopolitan at the time.
And so it-- we kept on going, and we did a lot of traveling.
All our summer vacations were always kept away from the home.
We more or less closed our home for the summer.
We went into all different areas.
We went to Hungary, to Siofok.
That's on the Balaton.
And we went skiing in the Alps and also skiing in the Tatra Mountains.
And--
Yes, yes, of course.
I had two governesses.
The-- when we were tiny little, my sister and I, we had a French governess, because my parents felt we should speak French.
So French became more or less another language to us.
And then as we grow older and we went into school, we had an English governess.
And believe it or not, I had English, and I spoke English fluently--
--when I arrived in the United States.
And also, the writing and spelling was fluent, and-- which was an incredible asset for us when we arrived, even though I never lost my accent, and I still have it to this day.
But English was my major language.
And during the time when we did a lot of traveling and all that, and my father also felt that if you learn a language you should go to the country.
So we spent a whole summer in England, and we spent times with a partner of my father, who was in partners in business with him.
And we lived with a family in England for three months in their home, and that was in 19-- in the summer of 1937.
And the agreement was that the following summer, their children would come stay with us and so they would learn German language, which, of course, never came to pass.
But we-- we came back from London.
And we also stayed in France for about three or four weeks with the same family.
And so it was delightful, because the languages were spoken, and it was something that we just enjoyed very much.
My sister-- even though we were very young, we were two little ladies.
We really were able to carry ourselves very well so we never were a burden to the family, so to speak.
Yes, 18 months difference, my sister and myself.
So during that period of time, we spent time together.
We were very close.
And then we returned to Vienna, and that year, that '37, '38, we went on an quite a long vacation to-- in the, I believe, the Hungarian mountains there skiing.
Because that was also a big thing with us was sports, gymnastic.
Yes, I skied, and I skated.
And we skated practically every day during the time we lived in Vienna.
My sister.
My sister.
We are very, very close to this very day.
And my parents, of course, were extremely close, which I will tell you a little later how it evolved in that.
And so basically, then we came back to Vienna after the wintertime and just continued our lives.
At that time, I went to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I--
In the school--
--yes.
There were mixed-- mixed religions.
But there were quite-- I would say the majority were Jewish children, maybe 50-50.
Because--
--it was an excellent school, very, very strict, extremely strict, which was what my--
Very strict.
The learning, the-- there were no chosen subjects.
There were 13 or 14 subjects, and these were the curriculum.
And you know, you started the school when you were 10 years old because you only had four years of elementary school.
Then you had a three-day exam that you had to take in order to be allowed to enter that school.
It was very strict and very intense learning.
Very hard.
We went to school six days a week.
My favorite subject?
I loved to do things with my hands.
I was very bad in math and Latin.
I hated Latin, but it was part of the curriculum.
I liked English.
English was part of it.
And you just-- I mean, there was such a thing that you failed.
You didn't fail in school.
Your parents saw to it that you-- you had tutors and you learned.
And so it was very, very hard.
Oh, absolutely, because you couldn't bring the shande on the family that you don't have a good education.
And my sister was brilliant, and she, of course, went to the same school as I did.
And the teachers always made the comparison-- how come that you have such a smart sister and you're so stupid?
So that really helped the child very much [LAUGHS] growing up, but that was the way it was.
And I remember one very funny incidence, just came to me.
I was in third year in high school, and I've got very bad midterm grades, and I really didn't know how to tell my father.
And I had to get the signature on his-- on, you know, to-- on the diploma, and I didn't know how to go about it.
And I sort of inched up to him and I says, papa, the most important thing is I'm healthy, and I handed him my report card.
And he couldn't get angry at me because the way I explained it to him.
So that's funny.
I just thought about it, thinking about school and, you know, doing this.
And as I said, schooling was uppermost.
Learning was uppermost.
Oh, I love to sew, and I love to do embroideries.
In fact, I have embroidery here at home, which I'll be happy to show you-- petit point and crocheting and knitting, which really came in very handy later on in my life.
And I always enjoyed doing that.
I also liked history, because it piqued my interest and to know how other people, you know, how they live.
And history was very important, and to this day, I enjoy it very much.
And basically, as I said, my sister also studied Japanese, because she was supposed to go to Japan for the Olympic Games as her graduation present.
And my father felt if you go to that country or any country, you better speak the language.
He did not believe that you should not speak languages.
And he spoke about three or four languages himself.
It was Polish, French fluently, German, and English haltingly, which, of course, he had to study later on and to learn it.
And--
Yes, I enjoy languages to this day.
I speak Spanish, enough to make myself understood.
Never went to school to learn Spanish, but Latin, as the basics of languages, helped me attain Spanish.
So I can make myself very easily understood.
And like I always said, you know, I speak loud but bad, and they understand me when I speak Spanish.
And I travel a lot to Mexico.
So based on all that, as what I was trying to explain, was that we had a very genteel life.
And sports, as I mentioned to you, were very important also.
I was gymnastics.
Looking at me today you wouldn't believe it, but I was a gymnast and skating and skiing.
And-- So you had a very rounded education--
Very rounded education.
Yes, very much so.
And my sister was more into studying and furthering herself.
I was more the playful kind, especially when they tell you you're stupid and she's brilliant.
In--
My dream for myself was to be happy.
I just wanted to be a happy-go-lucky kid.
And you never-- my father always wanted me to go into business with him, and that was his hope that I would be.
My sister was going to be the studious one.
She was supposed to go into the foreign corps, and that was his aim for her.
But he thought that I should go into business with him.
And what did I know about business at that time?
All I wanted to do was have fun.
Well, all that stopped on March the 13th. 19--
We were all--
OK.
All right.
Yes, sort of.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I would like to talk about it.
My mother was a very beautiful woman who enjoyed traveling.
And my father enjoyed life very much, and so I guess he had a few girlfriends on the side.
And he didn't have any problem not being able to afford them, and so he was able to afford that luxury.
No, we were never made aware of it.
The home life was always-- you know, papa and mama were always there.
And we didn't know what went on behind the scenes, and we didn't realize it until later on in life.
My sister may have been aware of it earlier than I was, because I didn't know it at the time.
And so my mother enjoyed traveling, and she did a lot of that.
And I guess she didn't want to be home that much to see, you know, of what.
And also the home was taken care of because we were well taken care of, and so she didn't have to worry about that.
And summer vacations were always-- my parents never spent their vacations together.
My father used to travel with his friends, and my mother traveled with us.
And usually we either rented a home somewhere.
It may have been in-- in Hungary on the-- on the-- what-- it was the Balaton.
And so we always rented something.
And she took the help with her, and we spent the summers away.
We locked up the apartment, and we just went away.
And then we came back, getting ready for school again and looking forward, especially my sister was already in dancing school, prior to Anschluss.
And so her social life started, and she had a-- she attended a ball.
You know, it was like the debutante's ball of the coming out.
And I was aching to be at that same point, and of course, I never attained that.
But--
Well, I had one young-- nice young man, and I still remember the very first kiss I got.
And we were ice skating, and all of a sudden he took me around and he gave me a kiss right on the ice.
And I thought it was just wonderful.
But yes, my sister had young men.
And she, in dancing school, they had-- they were able to have some kind of social life.
But it was very, very sheltered and very-- always very close to the family, not like the children have it today.
It's a different life today.
But you know, we live and learn, and we go through different eras in our lives, and you have to accept, I guess.
You don't-- you may not like it, but you have to learn to accept.
And our lives were different.
And how else can I--
Much more chaperoned, much more.
You knew exactly-- or your mother knew exactly every minute of what went on.
And I still remember the one time when my mother gave me the back of her hand because of a dirty word that I had picked up in school.
And I simply said it to her, but I'll-- I never said it again.
There was absolutely no abuse in the household.
I think it was shit, scheisse in Deutsche.
And it was just terrible.
I mean, you just didn't utter that word.
And--
Never.
All my father ever had to do was look at-- at my sister and me, and we died.
If he looked at us in a stern manner, that was it.
We absolutely melted and died and crawled within ourselves.
No, we were never, ever hit.
And one day my father talked to me about it.
And he said, I was beaten so terribly as a child because my father believed that you cannot only love, you also have to punish.
And he punished me so severely by beating me that I swore when I have children, never will I lay a hand on them.
And that was the truth.
He never ever did.
And for many, many years, he held it against his father, that he did that to him.
And for all the Jewish holidays, we always went to visit the relatives, his father in Poland.
For the high holidays, for Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah we went there.
And I remember once or twice we also went for Pesach, for Passover.
And I still remember the synagogue in Zywiec, where the ladies were all sitting upstairs, and the men were sitting downstairs.
And the men were all dressed in white when it came during Yom Kippur.
And the women used to bring the apples, and they-- they put little things into the apples to smell them so that your hunger wouldn't be so big during that time.
So you remember that.
And I remember the young man that used to blow-- blow the shofar so that nobody could wait that the-- the sun would set so he can blow it so everybody could go home and eat.
So these are some of the memories that I have.
And I-- my grandfather was a very, very tall man.
He was about 6 foot 2'', but he had infantile paralysis, which nobody could realize at that time, and he always needed help in walking.
And he always called me my jolly joker, because I was such a happy-go-lucky child, and he used to show me cards.
We used to play cards.
And he says, you know something?
You're the joker out of this deck.
So funny, I just thought about it.
Did he say it in Polish?
He-- he spoke German and Polish.
So that's--
[NON-ENGLISH] means please.
[NON-ENGLISH], good-bye.
No, the joker, the joker that [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, the joker.
That he called me?
Oh, because I was such a happy-go-lucky kid, and I always made-- I--
Joker, jolly joker.
In English.
In English.
It was really amazing.
But yes, he called me my jolly-- mein jolly joker.
So he must have picked this up somewhere.
I don't know where.
But he was a very learned man, and people came from all around to study the Talmud with him on, you know, at-- at-- at given times.
And so I did see a lot of very Orthodox living ways in Zywiec during that period of time.
And I remember-- oh, my god, it just came to me.
When they-- when they took a chicken and they killed it, and I think they call it-- where they-- they spin it around in the real Orthodox and the koshering.
And I saw this chicken jumping around without a head, and I said, I'll never do that again in my life ever.
How can you do that to a poor little chicken?
But that's how it was.
It was very rural living in Zywiec.
I mean, it was a small little town.
And I believe they had an outhouse, if I remember correctly.
So we only went there because my mother felt that she wanted us to have my grandfather see us.
And--
And the chicken--
--still was running around.
And I-- how can you do that to a poor little chicken?
But that's, I guess, that's the last living moments of a chicken.
But that's how they did it.
Why?
I don't know, but that's how they did it.
And I used to enjoy going to Poland.
And we also went to Cracow quite a bit because my father's brothers and sisters were living in Cracow.
And of course, I had all my cousins there that I adored, and we used to play together, and we had a good time together.
And the governess always went with us.
So we tried to sneak away a little bit and to get-- get away and still play our games.
But it was a very, very enjoyable time.
Oh, yes, I did.
His name was Yamek.
Yes, it was Yamek.
Unfortunately, he was killed.
But there were three others, and only one of them is alive now.
And-- but the three others were able-- which I will tell you later about how they escaped and made a life for themselves through the Polish underground.
It was quite-- quite interesting that portion too.
And my sister and I, oh, we used to fight like cats and dogs a little because there was so little difference in age.
But it was always said, well, she's the big one, and you're the little one, so you can't do the things she does.
And I had to live with that and live in the shadow of that.
And I used to say to myself, one day I'm going to grow up, and I'll be able to do all these things, which I did.
I really did in my-- in my lifetime.
I was able to do it.
[SIGHS]
